sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Gregory, presents with a nonhealing foot ulcer on his right foot, which has been present for approximately two months.
He reports that the ulcer has recently become red and is draining.
He denies any trauma to the foot, but believes he may have gotten the ulcer from walking barefoot on the beach at Dewey Beach.
His primary care physician (PCP) had started him on antibiotics, but he has not yet picked them up from the pharmacy.
He reports occasional throbbing pain and redness around the ulcer.
His biggest concern is the ulcer's failure to heal.
He has a history of diabetes, which he believes is a major contributor to the ulcer's poor healing.
Past medical history: The patient has diabetes, with an A1c of 10, which he believes his PCP discussed with him.
He has not checked his blood sugar regularly and does not know his current level.
He does not recall the name of the antibiotics prescribed by his PCP.
Review of systems: The patient reports occasional throbbing pain and redness around the ulcer.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's vital signs are normal.
Physical exam: A one-by-two inch circular wound is present on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the fifth metatarsal phalangeal joint.
The wound is red, swollen, and has drainage present.
There is edema around the wound, but no necrosis, odor, or bony exposure.
The patient reports increased pressure when the wound is touched, but no distinct pain.
Test Results: An x-ray of the right foot showed no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer with signs of infection.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; suspected infection; poorly controlled diabetes; [NOT MENTIONED]
Plan:
I prescribed clindamycin 400 milligrams, taken twice a day for seven days to treat the infection.
I will perform a debridement to remove dead tissue from the ulcer.
I will order an ABI (ankle-brachial index) to determine the blood supply to the foot.
I will obtain a surgical shoe for the patient to wear to reduce pressure on the foot and promote healing.
The patient will return in two weeks for follow-up.
Follow-up: The patient will return in two weeks for follow-up, at which time we will reassess the ulcer and consider another debridement.
